text: The lower esophageal sphincter or LES is a one-way valve that separates the esophagus from the stomach. When food or liquid is swallowed, the LES opens transiently to allow passage into the stomach.
Otherwise, the sphincter remains closed to prevent stomach contents from flowing back up into the esophagus.
However, for patients who suffer from reflux, the LES abnormally remains open allowing stomach secretions and contents to reflux up into the esophagus causing symptoms.
A hiatal hernia may also be present further promoting abnormal reflux.
A hiatal hernia is when the top of the stomach gets displaced or herniates through the diaphragm and up into the chest cavity.
Given this anatomic shift, the overall esophageal length is now shorter.
Additionally, the lower esophageal sphincter is no longer supported by the diaphragm to help with its sphincteric function.
Normally, the LES is positioned at the same level as the diaphragm.
In order to maximally control reflux surgically, both the hiatal hernia and the abnormally relaxed LES must be corrected.
The surgeon begins the repair of the hiatal hernia, repositioning the herniated portion of the stomach back below the diaphragm into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon pulls the stomach back into its normal position below the diaphragm and reduces the size of the diaphragmatic opening to repair the hiatal hernia.
The surgeon places a mesh over the incision site to reinforce the closure, aiming to minimize the risk of hiatal hernia recurrence.
The surgeon tightens the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) by wrapping the fundus of the stomach around it, resembling a belt-like structure.
The surgeon completes a 360-degree wrap of the stomach around the lower esophageal sphincter, a procedure known as Nissen fundoplication.
The video shows the surgical repair of a hiatal hernia, with the stomach being repositioned below the diaphragm and the diaphragmatic opening being reduced in size.
The surgeon dissects the stomach, esophagus, and hernia sac from the surrounding chest and abdominal soft tissue structures, carefully separating these anatomical components.
During the mobilization of the hernia sac and esophagus, the surgeon carefully identifies the vagal nerves, ensuring they are not injured during the dissection process.
The surgeon mobilizes the esophagus, ensuring that at least three centimeters of the distal esophagus are positioned within the abdominal cavity to prevent recurrence of the hiatal hernia.
The surgeon performs a high mediastinal dissection to achieve the necessary length of the esophagus for the procedure.
The surgeon closes the enlarged diaphragmatic opening, or hiatus, with sutures, ensuring a tension-free repair.
The surgeon places a mesh over the closed diaphragmatic hiatus incision to reinforce the repair and minimize the risk of hiatal hernia recurrence.
The esophagus and stomach are visibly freed from surrounding tissues, allowing for surgical manipulation during the procedure.
A rubber band is looped around the lower esophageal sphincter (LES), with the esophagus positioned above and the stomach below, visually demarcating these anatomical structures during the procedure.
The white mesh is visible at the top of the surgical field, indicating the area where a hiatal hernia has been repaired.
The surgeon pushes the top part of the stomach behind and around the esophagus, visually wrapping it to create a fundoplication.
The surgeon moves the stomach back and forth to ensure that the surrounding tissues are adequately loosened and mobile.
If it is too tight, blood flow can become restricted resulting in tissue damage.
The surgeon sutures the stomach wrap around the lower esophagus, securing the fundoplication in place.
The surgeon places the first suture, connecting stomach tissue to stomach tissue, as part of the stomach wrap procedure.
The surgeon places the second suture through the esophagus, securing the stomach wrap around the esophagus.
The surgeon places the final third suture through the lower esophageal sphincter (LES), completing the suturing process.
The surgeon completes the Nissen fundoplication, finalizing the suturing of the stomach wrap around the lower esophagus.
